coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus doped carbon nanodots particles based on pomegranate peels for highly active dehydrogenation of sodium borohydride in methanol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: GCGW-21 - Global Warming Reduction by Hydrogen
coredata.dc:creator: Samatya Ölmez, Saba
Balbay, Asım
Saka, Cafer
coredata.dc:description: Here, the carbon nanodots were successfully synthesized from pomegranate peels (PPCD). This obtained PPCD was treated by a hydrothermal process with phosphoric acid for P doping (P doped PPCD) and used as a metal-free catalyst to obtain hydrogen(H2) from sodium borohydride (NaBH4) methanolysis for the first time. The characteristics of the samples obtained by ultraviolet, fluorescence, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analyses were examined. NaBH4 concentration effect, temperature effect and catalyst reusability experiments were carried out. Using 10 mg of the catalyst with 2.5% NaBH4, an HGR value of 13000 mL min−1g−1 was obtained. The activation energy (Ea) for the P-doped PPCD catalyst was 30.96 kJ mol−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pomegranate peels
Carbon nanodots
P doping
Sodium borohydride
Hydrogen

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: GCGW-21 - Global Warming Reduction by Hydrogen

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectrum of PPCD (daylight (A) and UV light (B)).
XRD patterns for pure PPCD and P-doped PPCD catalysts at 2θ = 10°–80°.
TEM images features of PPCD(a) and P doped PPCD(b).
FTIR spectroscopy was performed to confirm the surface chemistry. The FT-IR spectra of PPCD and P-doped PPCD in the range of 400–4000 cm−1(a) and 400–2000 cm−1(b).
H2 volumes resulting from the NaBH4 methanolysis reaction with 10 mg of PPCD and P-doped PPCD catalysts at 30 °C.
The effect of different NaBH4 ratios (1.25%, 2.5%, 3.75% and 5%) on the methanolysis reaction at 30 °C.
The effect of temperature (30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C) on the methanolysis reaction of metal-free P doped PPCD(10 mg) and 2.5% NaBH4.
Arrhenius graph for P doped PPCD.
Reusability experiments for H2 production from this methanolysis reaction with P doped PPCD catalyst.
Comparison of HGR and Ea values of catalysts used for the production of H2 from NaBH4 in methanol.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus doped carbon nanodots particles based on pomegranate peels for highly active dehydrogenation of sodium borohydride in methanol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Siirt University, Faculty of Engineering, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Siirt University, Faculty of Engineering, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Arapgir Vocational School, Malatya Turgut Ozal University, Malatya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Arapgir Vocational School, Malatya Turgut Ozal University, Malatya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Health Sciences, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Health Sciences, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, the carbon nanodots were successfully synthesized from pomegranate peels (PPCD). This obtained PPCD was treated by a hydrothermal process with phosphoric acid for P doping (P doped PPCD) and used as a metal-free catalyst to obtain hydrogen(H2) from sodium borohydride (NaBH4) methanolysis for the first time. The characteristics of the samples obtained by ultraviolet, fluorescence, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analyses were examined. NaBH4 concentration effect, temperature effect and catalyst reusability experiments were carried out. Using 10 mg of the catalyst with 2.5% NaBH4, an HGR value of 13000 mL min−1g−1 was obtained. The activation energy (Ea) for the P-doped PPCD catalyst was 30.96 kJ mol−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the studies on alternative energy sources in terms of excessive use of fossil fuels and the damage they cause to the environment, hydrogen is considered the energy carrier of the future. Hydrogen storage is one of the important issues today. NaBH4, which is among the chemical hydrides for hydrogen storage, has a significant interest by researchers with its advantages such as being non-flammable or dangerous, having a gravimetric hydrogen storage capacity of 10.8% and being environmentally friendly [1–4]. However, the hydrolysis of NaBH4 in the presence of a catalyst has disadvantages such as slow reaction rates and unsuitable at low temperatures [5,6].

The alcoholysis reactions of NaBH4 have attracted great interest as an alternative to the NaBH4 hydrolysis system. Because of its ability to produce hydrogen below zero and its high reactivity, methanol is the most preferred alcohol in the alcoholysis reactions of NaBH4 [7]. In addition, tetra methoxy borate, a by-product of the NaBH4 methanolysis reaction, can easily polymerize into polyborates, preventing precipitation that causes pipe clogging and catalyst poisoning [8].

One of the ways to overcome this disadvantage is to use methanol as a solvent instead of water as given in Equation (1). As a result of using methanol instead of water, both an increase in the reaction rate and easy operation at low temperatures can be achieved.
(1) NaBH4+4CH3OH→NaB(OCH3)4+4H2

Choosing an appropriate type of catalyst is important for methanol reactions of NaBH4. Homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts are widely used to increase hydrogen production from NaBH4. In general, metal catalysts such as Pt, and Pd exhibit significant catalytic activity in the formation of H2 from this hydrolysis or methanolysis reaction, while their high cost and disposal of metal catalysts into the environment after use limit their use in practical applications. Therefore, nowadays, researchers have turned to low-cost and efficient catalysts [4,9–11].

With the rapid development of the world economy, environmental pollution and energy have become one important issue. For this reason, studies on green synthesis methods and clean and sustainable energy have become important. Most researchers work for the most effective use of natural and sustainable resources. In particular, fluorescent carbon quantum dots; bioimaging, chemical sensors, solar cells, nanomedicine, electrocatalysis and light-emitting diode have recently been the focus of widespread attention. However, carbon nanoparticles, also known as carbon nanodots, have attracted great attention due to their semiconductivity, special morphology, superior water solubility, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, chemically inert and high luminescence properties. In the last 10 years, numerous methods have been presented for the carbon dots synthesis, which can be divided into two types; top-down and bottom-up approaches. Both methods have problems such as aggregation, homogeneity, particle size control and surface properties during the carbonization process. Solutions such as excellent synthetic pathways and post-synthetic treatments are offered to solve these problems. Carbon dots can be synthesized from a variety of precursors such as chemical, green and waste materials, and many natural sources of carbon [12–18]. The carbon-containing small molecules, such as carbohydrates or organic acid, are converted to carbon dots. In addition, an important array of polysaccharides such as starch and chitosan has been used for the carbon dots synthesis for monosaccharides such as glucose and sucrose. Different research groups suggest that eggshell ash, orange juice, soy milk, willow bark, pomelo bark, glycerol, etc. have sought to synthesize carbon dots from natural carbon sources [19–23].

Here, carbon nanodots synthesized from pomegranate peels treated by a hydrothermal process with phosphoric acid were used as a metal-free catalyst to obtain H2 from NaBH4 methanolysis for the first time. The characteristics of the samples obtained by ultraviolet, fluorescence, XRD, FTIR, TEM and ICP-MS analyses were examined.
All chemical reagents of analytical quality were used without further purification. Urea (CO(NH2)2), phosphoric acid (H3PO43), NaBH4 were all purchased from Merck.

In this study, Zivzik pomegranate peel from the Siirt region of Turkey was used as a carbon precursor. The pomegranate peels obtained from the local region were first cut into small pieces to remove moisture content and dried in sunlight. After the sun-dried pomegranate peel pieces were ground, 50 g of ground pomegranate peels were taken into a mini ceramic container and burned at different temperatures and times by thermal method. To obtain fluorescent solutions under UV light, they were carbonized at 200, 300 and 400 °C and for 4 h in preliminary trials. The resulting carbonized pomegranate peels were dispersed in ultrapure water and sonicated for 15 min. The sonicated brown solution was then filtered through a 0.25-μm membrane to obtain a supernatant solution containing carbon dots.

The formation of carbon dots was tested with a UV lamp at 365 nm. In the preliminary trials of the thermal method aqueous carbon source solution, it was determined that the carbonization process did not occur at 200 °C, and the solid obtained at 400 °C was not soluble in water. The carbon dots extracted at 300 °C heat treatment exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV light 365 nm. The supernatant solution was evaporated in an oven at 85 °C. The final powder product was made ready to be used in the catalyst stage.

For the preparation of P doped PPCD, approximately 0.25 g of PPCD sample into a 50 mL beaker was taken. Then, H3PO4 solution (30 mL, diluted with 1:4) into the beaker was added. This mixture was kept in a magnetic stirrer at 50 °C at 60 rpm stirring speed for 5 h. After the obtained sample was filtered, it was washed with distilled water. After, the sample in an oven (80 °C) was dried. The sample as P-doped PPCD was labelled.

The TEM images of PPCD and P-doped PPCD catalysts were performed on the JEM-2100F (JEOL). XRD analysis for the PPCD and P-doped PPCD using Cu-Kα radiation by λ = 1.5416 A) on a Rigaku D/max-2550 model device was taken. FTIR analyses (Nicolet 6700 spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were taken in the 400-4000 cm−1 range. The P content in the P doped PPCD sample was determined by Agilent 7800 model Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) equipped with a mass spectrometer. Absorption spectra of the carbon dot sample were obtained with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Biochrom Libra S22). The fluorescence property of the carbon dot sample was examined with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000, Japan).

A water displacement method was used to produce H2 with these metal-free catalysts. In the experimental stage, firstly, 0.125 g of NaBH4 and 10 mg of metal-free catalyst were added to a reaction flask and placed in a water bath. After the temperature of the water bath was adjusted, methanol (10 mL) into the reaction flask was injected. H2 produced over time without any mixing was recorded. HGR values were determined based on 80% of the theoretically expected H2 volume. For reusability tests, five consecutive experiments were performed by injecting 10 mL of methanol into the reaction flask containing 10 mg of catalyst and 0.125 g of NaBH4. After the first experiment, 0.125 g of NaBH4 was again added to the reaction flask and the experiments were performed five times.

The carbon dots characteristically exhibit strong absorption in the UV region, with an absorption shoulder or tail extending into the visible range. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence capability of PPCDs is shown from a 10–15 cm distance under normal room light (A) and UV light (B). The light brown PPCDs emit strong blue light when viewed under a lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. According to the UV–vis spectrum, the PPCDs showed maximum absorbance at 320 nm.

One of the most distinctive features of carbon dots is their photoluminescence emissions. In most published papers, a common feature of photoluminescences from carbon dots is the excitation dependence on PL emission wavelengths and intensities [24,25]. The emission spectra of the carbon dots were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in the fluorescence spectra graph given in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence properties of PPCDs continue to decrease in a wide wavelength range up to 800 nm when excitation is made in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm. Emission spectra were taken with different excitation wavelengths. The strong fluorescent emission band at 440 nm was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

XRD patterns for pure PPCD and P-doped PPCD catalysts at 2θ = 10°–80° are presented in Fig. 3 . For the PPCD catalyst, the typical cellulose peak was observed at approximately 2θ = 17.7° [26]. However, the characteristic peak belonging to the cellulose structure for the P-doped PPCD catalyst disappeared after treatment with H3PO4. The two diffraction peaks of the PPCD sample at 24.2 (002) and 41°'(100), respectively, correspond to the graphite lattice spacing [27]. At the same time, the peak at about 24.2° slipped to 23.8°. Also, the peaks at 2θ = 28.58° and 49.97° for the PPCD catalyst can be attributed to the presence of K2O and K2CO3 (JCPDS No. 77–2176 and 87–0730). Peaks at 2θ = 28.40° and 53.03° can be assigned to CaO and CaCO3 (JCPDS No. 47–1743, 37–1497). Also, the peaks at 2θ = 43.92° and 65.31° can be interpreted as MgO (JCPDS No. 04–0829, 75–1525). A similar result was expressed in another study [28]. However, as a result of hydrothermal treatment with H3PO4, the peaks of CaO, CaCO3, K2O, K2CO3 and MgO disappeared in the XRD pattern obtained for the P doped PPCD catalyst. As a result, it can be stated that the crystal structure of the PPCD catalyst turns into an amorphous structure after hydrothermal treatment with H3PO4.

Morphological features of PPCD(a) and P doped PPCD(b) were observed using TEM and the images obtained are given in Fig. 4 . The TEM image of the synthesized PPCD shows that the particles are nearly spherical and well dispersed without any visible aggregation. The mean particle size of PPCD was found to be 2.3 nm. After interaction with H3PO4, some aggregate species were found on the PPCD surface. The mean particle value for the P doped PPCD was 11.9 nm. When the particle sizes of the P doped PPCD samples are compared, there is an increase in the average particle sizes with the inclusion of phosphorus in the PPCD structure.

Morphological features of PPCD(a) and P doped PPCD(b) were observed using TEM and the images obtained are given in Fig. 4. The TEM image of the synthesized PPCD shows that the particles are nearly spherical and well dispersed without any visible aggregation. The mean particle size of PPCD was found to be 2.3 nm. After interaction with H3PO4, some aggregate species were found on the PPCD surface. The mean particle value for the P doped PPCD was 11.9 nm. As a result of the addition of phosphoric acid, the particles on the catalyst probably interacted more with each other, causing agglomeration. When the particle sizes of the P doped PPCD samples are compared, there is an increase in the average particle sizes with the inclusion of phosphorus in the PPCD structure.

Carbon dots are known to be rich in surface functional groups and graphite-like C–H2 linkages. Carbon dots characterized by sp2 are composed of different functional groups on their surface such as carbonyl, ether, epoxy, amino, carboxylic acid and a hydroxyl group. The presence of such groups on the C points leads to their high hydrophilicity. With an average size below 10 nm, the structural properties of carbon dots are similar to nanocrystalline graphite having rich is sp2 hybridized C–C linkages [25]. Typically, carbon dots contain oxidized moieties such as carboxyl on their surface. FTIR spectroscopy was performed to confirm the surface chemistry. The FT-IR spectra of PPCD and P-doped PPCD in the range of 400–4000 cm−1(a) and 400–2000 cm−1(b) are presented in Fig. 5 . Bands around 3000-3600 cm−1 can be assigned to the hydroxylic group stretching vibration. Peaks in the range of 1650–1050 cm−1 can be assigned to vibrational absorption of C–O and CO [29]. The main absorption bands occurring at 702, 829, 1386 and 1584 cm−1 for PPCD (a) support the presence of metal carbonates such as K2CO3, CaCO3, and MgCO3 in the sample structure. However, the FTIR spectrum of the P-doped PPCD sample is quite different from the PPCD sample. This is because of the change in both the pore structure of the PPCD sample after H3PO4 treatment by hydrothermal treatment and the corresponding change in the binding of various functional groups by P doping. The new peak at 1724 cm−1 for P doped PPCD can be assigned to CO stretching vibrations. The peak corresponding to the P–O–C stretching vibrations occurred at ∼946 cm−1 [30,31]. Also, the peak at 1085 cm−1 for P-doped PPCD may be related to ionized P+ O− or P–O–C vibrations [32,33] Consistent with the XRD results, most of the peaks at 600-1000 cm−1 for PPCD disappeared after hydrothermal treatment with H3PO4. The FTIR spectrum revealed that there are important functional groups in the structure of P-doped PPCD. In addition, ICP-MS analysis was also performed to prove that the P atom is doped in the PPCD structure. According to the results of the ICP-MS analysis, the percentage of P atoms found in the PPCD sample was 1.37. This result indicates that the P atom is doped in the PPCD structure.

Fig. 6 shows the volumes of H2 resulting from the NaBH4 methanolysis reaction with 10 mg of PPCD and P-doped PPCD catalysts at 30 °C. The end times of this reaction with PPCD and P doped PPCD catalysts were found to be 420 and 300 s, respectively. At the same time, HGR values of 5555 and 7714 mL min−1 g−1, respectively, were obtained with the same catalysts. However, the finish time of this reaction was 840 s with only methanol under the same conditions as 1.25% NaBH4. As can be understood, the PPCD and P doped PPCD catalysts used accelerated the reaction significantly. In NaBH4 hydrolysis reactions in the presence of a catalyst, the Bimolecular Langmuir-Hinshelwood model is generally used to evaluate the reaction mechanism [34]. With a similar approach, the mechanism of the metal-free catalysts used in this study on the reaction for H2 release from NaBH4 methanolysis can also be evaluated.

BH4 − ions in the methanol medium are adsorbed on the charged surface of the metal-free catalyst formed by P doping. As a result of electron transfer, H− is formed. That is, an activated complex is formed as a result of interactions between NaBH4 and metal-free catalysts. CH3OH adsorbs to form activated complex-H. This activated complex-H reacts with the protonic hydrogen of CH3OH to give 1 equivalent of H2. At the same time, CH3O− and BH3 react in solution to produce BH3OCH3 −. This reaction continues until NaB(OCH3)4 and a total of 4H2 are produced [35]. Therefore, the more easily BH4 − can be adsorbed in the active site of the surface, the faster the reaction with the free H of CH3OH. Thus, hydrogen can be formed in a shorter time with an accelerated reaction rate. That is, the presence of a charged metal-free catalyst can significantly improve catalytic performance by accelerating the electron transfer process. Thus, the electron-rich and electron-deficient surface regions formed on this catalyst surface are very important for the NaBH4 methanolysis reaction steps [36].

The effect of different ratios (1.25%, 2.5%, 3.75% and 5%) of NaBH4 on the methanolysis reaction was performed and the resulting H2 volumes are given in Fig. 7 . HGR values of 7714, 13250, 17600 and 19666 mL min−1 g−1 were obtained, respectively, with the amounts of 1.25%, 2.5%, 3.75% and 5% NaBH4. It should be noted that there is an approximately 2-fold raise in the HGR value by increasing the NaBH4 amount from 1.25% to 2.5%. However, the same increase was not observed in the HGR value obtained by increasing the amount of NaBH4 from 2.5% to 5%. The increase in the viscosity of solution by NaBH4 amount is a possible reason for this decrease. Also, the H2 generation rate decreases as a result of the blocking of the active sites of this metal-free catalyst due to the increase in the concentration of BH4 − ions [37].

The temperature effect (30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C) on the methanolysis reaction of metal-free P doped PPCD (10 mg) and 2.5% NaBH4 was tested and the results are given in Fig. 8 . The end times of the methanolysis reaction of NaBH4 at 30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C were 300, 180, and 120 s, respectively. At the same time, the maximum HGRs obtained for the same temperatures were found to be 7714, 13000, and 18000 mL min−1 g−1, respectively. A comparison of the HGR values with the P doped PPCD catalyst and the catalysts used in the NaBH4 methanolysis studies in the literature are given in Table 1 . The HGR obtained with the P doped PPCD appears to be higher than the HGR values of many of the other metal-free catalysts given in Table 1.

The corresponding Arrhenius equation for this reaction is shown below.
(2) ln(k) = −Ea/RT + ln(A)
where k is the hydrogen production rate constant, Ea is the activation energy, A is the pro-exponential factor, T is the solution temperature, and R is the gas constant. By plotting the value of lnk against 1/T, Ea can be obtained by the slope of the resulting line as shown in Fig. 9 . The Ea value calculated with P doped PPCD is 30.96 kJ mol−1. This value is lower than the Ea values given in Table 1 [38–46].

The reusability tests for P doped PPCD catalyst were performed five times in succession under the same conditions and the results are given in Fig. 10 . Reusability experiments were performed at 30 °C with 10 mg of catalyst, 10 mL of methanol and 1.25% NaBH4. At the end of each experiment, these tests were performed by adding 1.25% NaBH4 into the reaction flask again under the same conditions after the H2 output was finished. At the end of each use, 335 mL of H2 was obtained, which should theoretically be produced. That is, 100% conversion has been achieved. However, there was a gradual decrease in catalytic activity. The possible reason for this result may be related to the sodium tetramethoxyborate concentration in the solution and the increase in the viscosity of the solution and the formation of insufficient catalytic active sites for NaBH4 methanolysis. It has been stated that tetra methoxy borate, a by-product of the methanolysis of NaBH4, causes a decrease in catalytic activity and decreases surface charges due to the displacement of H+ ions on this catalyst [41]. At the same time, it has been reported that there is a decrease in catalytic activity with alkalinization of the solution environment due to the accumulation of this by-product over time [47].”

The small size (<10 nm in size), good conductivity, and rapid electron transfer of carbon dots provide the carbon dots-based composite with developed electric conductivity and catalytic activity. The carbon dots have abundant functional groups on the surface which could facilitate the preparation of active catalysts. In the study, carbon nanodots synthesized from pomegranate peels by a hydrothermal process with phosphoric acid were used for the first time as a metal-free catalyst to obtain H2 from NaBH4 methanolysis. The characteristics of the samples obtained by ultraviolet, fluorescence, XRD, FTIR, TEM and ICP-MS analyzes were examined. The crystal structure of the PPCD catalyst turns into an amorphous structure after hydrothermal treatment with H3PO4. The TEM image of the synthesized PPCD shows that the particles are nearly spherical and well dispersed without any visible aggregation. After interaction with H3PO4, some aggregate species were found on the PPCD surface. FTIR spectrum of the P-doped PPCD sample is quite different from the PPCD sample because of the change in both the pore structure of the PPCD sample after H3PO4 treatment by hydrothermal treatment and the corresponding change in the binding of various functional groups by P doping. According to the results of the ICP-MS analysis, the percentage of P atoms found in the PPCD sample was 1.37. Using 10 mg of the catalyst with 2.5% NaBH4, an HGR value of 13000 mL min−1g−1 was obtained. The Ea for the P-doped PPCD catalyst was 30.96 kJ mol−1. This work also shows that it offers potential for work to expand doping with metal-free elements to carbon dots to produce water-soluble, green catalysts and improve light absorption ability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by Siirt University Scientific Research Unit (2020-SİÜMÜH-006).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
